title: Socrates | Biography, Philosophy, Method, Death, & Facts
text:
Socrates | Biography, Philosophy, Method, Death, & Facts

Section Title: Socrates > Who was Socrates? > What did Socrates teach?
Content:
Socrates professed not to teach anything (and indeed not to know anything important) but only to seek answers to urgent human questions (e.g., “What is virtue?” and “What is justice?”) and to help others do the same. His style of philosophizing was to engage in public conversations about some human excellence and, through skillful questioning, to show that his interlocutors did not know what they were talking about. Despite the negative results of these encounters, Socrates did hold some broad positive views, including that virtue is a form of knowledge and that “care of the soul” (the cultivation of virtue) is the most important human obligation.

Section Title: Socrates > Philosophical and literary sources
Content:
Soon after Socrates’ death, several members of his circle preserved and praised his memory by [writing](https://www.britannica.com/topic/writing) works that represent him in his most characteristic activity—conversation. His [interlocutors](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/interlocutors) in these (typically adversarial) exchanges included people he happened to meet, devoted followers, prominent political figures, and leading thinkers of the day. Many of these “Socratic discourses,” as [Aristotle](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Aristotle) calls them in his *Poetics* , are no longer extant; there are only brief remnants of the conversations written by [Antisthenes](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Antisthenes) , [Aeschines](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Aeschines) , [Phaedo](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Phaedo-Greek-philosopher) , and Eucleides. But those composed by Plato and Xenophon sur

Section Title: Socrates > [Xenophon](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Xenophon)
Content:
Among the compositions of [Xenophon](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Xenophon) , the one that gives the fullest portrait of Socrates is *[Memorabilia](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Memorabilia-by-Xenophon)* . The first two chapters of Book I of this work are especially important, because they explicitly undertake a refutation of the charges made against Socrates at his trial; they are therefore a valuable supplement to Xenophon’s *Apology* , which is devoted entirely to the same purpose. The portrait of Socrates that Xenophon gives in Books III and IV of *Memorabilia* seems, in certain passages, to be heavily influenced by his reading of some of Plato’s dialogues, and so the evidentiary [value](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/value) of at least this portion of the work is diminished.

Section Title: Socrates > Who was Socrates? > Why didn’t Socrates try to escape his death sentence?
Content:
**Socrates** (born c. 470 bce , [Athens](https://www.britannica.com/place/Athens) [Greece]—died 399 bce , Athens) was an [ancient Greek](https://www.britannica.com/place/ancient-Greece) [philosopher](https://www.britannica.com/topic/philosophy) whose way of life, character, and thought exerted a profound influence on [Classical antiquity](https://www.britannica.com/event/Classical-antiquity) and [Western philosophy](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Western-philosophy) .
